questions.prompt: What's the right heuristic for deciding when to vertically integrate?

choices.text: Own what controls product quality, cost, and cycle time; buy commodities and items where suppliers have insurmountable advantages. Vertical wins when iteration speed + supply risk + proprietary process matter more than per-unit cost optimization. Loses on commodities, slow-changing components, or when capital is constrained.

questions.explanation: Vertical integration is a deliberate choice per component, not a universal strategy. Match the choice to where you have strategic advantage and where the suppliers don't. Tesla's depth in batteries / motors / software is justified by cycle time and supply risk; their use of commodity tires and basic parts uses suppliers because no strategic gain exists.

questions.prompt: A startup making electric scooters in 2026 considers vertically integrating their battery pack manufacturing 'like Tesla.' Right call?

choices.text: Probably not. The 2026 EV battery supplier ecosystem is mature; commodity packs are available cheaply. Vertical integration would burn capital with no strategic gain — speed and cost are already optimized by the ecosystem. Tesla's vertical battery work in 2010 was forced (no suppliers existed); 2026 is different. Copy Tesla's reasoning ('own what matters strategically'), not the literal playbook.
Outsource everything and add Tesla logos.

questions.explanation: Vertical integration decisions are context-dependent. Tesla in 2010 had no choice on batteries. A 2026 startup has many choices. Burn capital on vertical integration only where you have a strategic advantage suppliers can't match.
